 {"id":"bd-1ak","title":"EPIC: ORM Relationship System","description":"# ORM Relationship System

## Overview
Implement full ORM relationship support comparable to SQLAlchemy/SQLModel. This is THE defining feature that separates an ORM from a mere query builder with type mapping.

## Why This Matters
Without relationships, users must:
- Manually write JOINs for every related query
- Manually track foreign keys
- Lose the core ergonomic benefit of an ORM (`hero.team` should just work)

Python SQLModel provides:
```python
class Hero(SQLModel, table=True):
    team_id: Optional[int] = Field(foreign_key="team.id")
    team: Optional["Team"] = Relationship(back_populates="heroes")

class Team(SQLModel, table=True):
    heroes: List["Hero"] = Relationship(back_populates="team")

# Usage: hero.team automatically fetches the related Team
```

## Rust Design Philosophy
Instead of runtime reflection magic, we use:
1. **Compile-time type safety** via proc macros
2. **Explicit relationship definitions** that generate correct code
3. **Optional lazy loading** (user opts in, not default)
4. **Eager loading support** for performance-conscious queries

## Target API (Rust)
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table = "heroes")]
struct Hero {
    #[sqlmodel(primary_key, auto_increment)]
    id: Option<i64>,
    
    #[sqlmodel(foreign_key = "teams.id")]
    team_id: Option<i64>,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship(model = "Team", foreign_key = "team_id"))]
    team: Related<Team>,  // or Lazy<Team> for lazy loading
}

#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table = "teams")]
struct Team {
    #[sqlmodel(primary_key, auto_increment)]
    id: Option<i64>,
    
    name: String,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship(model = "Hero", back_populates = "team"))]
    heroes: RelatedMany<Hero>,
}
```

## Success Criteria
- [ ] One-to-many relationships work (Team has many Heroes)
- [ ] Many-to-one relationships work (Hero belongs to Team)
- [ ] Many-to-many with link tables work
- [ ] Eager loading with JOINs works
- [ ] Optional lazy loading works
- [ ] back_populates synchronization works
- [ ] Cascade operations work via FK constraints
- [ ] Type-safe at compile time

## Architecture Decisions
1. **Related<T> vs Lazy<T>**: Two wrapper types - Related for eager-loaded, Lazy for deferred
2. **No automatic lazy loading by default**: Explicit is better for Rust, prevents N+1 surprises
3. **Macro-generated accessors**: The relationship macro generates the fetch methods
4. **Connection context required**: Must pass connection/session to load related data

## Dependencies
- Core Model trait (done)
- Query builder with JOINs (done)
- Session/Unit-of-Work (separate epic, can work without but better with)

## Estimated Scope
- 8-12 subtasks
- Touches: sqlmodel-core, sqlmodel-macros, sqlmodel-query
- New types: Related<T>, RelatedMany<T>, Lazy<T>, RelationshipInfo","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-27T20:12:54.238244618Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T00:04:07.379475967Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T00:04:07.379414012Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-1cr","title":"Field Attributes: Complete All 40+ Parameters","description":"## Overview

Implement ALL Field() parameters from Python SQLModel/Pydantic.

## Python Field() Parameters (Complete List)

### Basic Parameters
- default: Any - Default value for field
- default_factory: Optional[Callable] - Factory function for defaults
- alias: Optional[str] - Input/output alias
- validation_alias: Optional[str] - Input-only alias (Pydantic v2)
- serialization_alias: Optional[str] - Output-only alias (Pydantic v2)
- title: Optional[str] - Field title in schema
- description: Optional[str] - Field description in schema
- exclude: Union[set, Mapping] - Exclude from model operations
- include: Union[set, Mapping] - Include in model operations
- schema_extra: Optional[dict] - Extra schema configuration
- const: Optional[bool] - Constant value constraint
- repr: bool - Include in repr (default: True)

### Validation Parameters
- gt, ge, lt, le: Optional[float] - Numeric bounds
- multiple_of: Optional[float] - Must be multiple of value
- max_digits: Optional[int] - Max decimal digits
- decimal_places: Optional[int] - Decimal precision
- min_items, max_items: Optional[int] - Collection size bounds
- unique_items: Optional[bool] - Items must be unique
- min_length, max_length: Optional[int] - String length bounds
- allow_mutation: bool - Allow field mutation
- regex: Optional[str] - Regex pattern validation
- discriminator: Optional[str] - Union type discrimination

### SQLAlchemy Column Parameters
- primary_key, nullable, unique, index - Column constraints
- sa_type - SQLAlchemy type override
- sa_column - Full SQLAlchemy Column (mutually exclusive with other SA params)
- sa_column_args, sa_column_kwargs - Extra Column() args/kwargs
- foreign_key, ondelete, onupdate - Foreign key config

## Current Status

Many are implemented but gaps exist in:
- Alias system (validation_alias, serialization_alias)
- Schema metadata (title, description, schema_extra)
- Collection validators (min_items, max_items, unique_items)
- Full sa_column override support
- multiple_of validation
- discriminator support

## Implementation Notes

Each parameter must:
1. Be parseable from #[sqlmodel(...)] or #[field(...)] attributes
2. Generate appropriate SQL DDL where applicable
3. Generate validation code where applicable
4. Work with serde for serialization
5. Have tests covering all edge cases","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:59:11.095624519Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T16:58:19.317457938Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1cr","depends_on_id":"bd-162","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T16:58:19.317440856Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1cuq","title":"Implement horizontal sharding","description":"## Description

Partition data across multiple databases.

## Python Behavior

```python
from sqlalchemy.ext.horizontal_shard import ShardedSession

class ShardedDB:
    def shard_chooser(self, mapper, instance, clause=None):
        if instance:
            return f'shard_{instance.region}'
        return 'shard_default'
    
    def id_chooser(self, query, ident):
        return ['shard_us', 'shard_eu']  # Search all
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
pub struct ShardedPool {
    shards: HashMap<String, Pool>,
    chooser: Box<dyn ShardChooser>,
}

pub trait ShardChooser: Send + Sync {
    fn choose_for_insert<M: Model>(&self, model: &M) -> String;
    fn choose_for_query(&self, query: &Query) -> Vec<String>;
}

// Example: shard by user_id modulo
struct ModuloShardChooser {
    shard_count: usize,
}

impl ShardChooser for ModuloShardChooser {
    fn choose_for_insert<M: Model>(&self, model: &M) -> String {
        let id = model.shard_key();
        format!("shard_{}", id % self.shard_count)
    }
}
```

## Shard Key

Models need shard key:
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(shard_key = "user_id")]
struct Order {
    user_id: i64,
    // ...
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ShardedPool implementation
- [ ] ShardChooser trait
- [ ] Shard key on models
- [ ] Cross-shard queries
- [ ] Shard-aware transactions

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/sharding.rs)
- [ ] Test shard key extraction
- [ ] Test shard routing function
- [ ] Test cross-shard query handling
- [ ] Test shard configuration

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/horizontal_sharding.rs)
- [ ] Query routes to correct shard
- [ ] Insert routes by shard key
- [ ] Cross-shard aggregation
- [ ] Shard rebalancing
- [ ] Shard failover

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Shard routing decision
- [ ] INFO: Shard selection
- [ ] WARN: Cross-shard query detected

-{"id":"bd-1cy","title":"Implement Window Functions (OVER clause)","description":"## Description

Support window functions with OVER clause.

## Python Behavior

```python
from sqlalchemy import func, over

# Row number
stmt = select(
    User.name,
    func.row_number().over(order_by=User.created_at).label('row_num')
)

# Partition by
stmt = select(
    Order.id,
    func.sum(Order.amount).over(partition_by=Order.customer_id).label('customer_total')
)

# Window frame
stmt = select(
    Sale.date,
    func.avg(Sale.amount).over(
        partition_by=Sale.product_id,
        order_by=Sale.date,
        rows=(-2, 0)  # Current row and 2 preceding
    )
)
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
let query = select!(User)
    .column(Expr::function("row_number", vec![])
        .over()
        .order_by(User::created_at.asc())
        .as_("row_num")
    );

let query = select!(Order)
    .column(Order::amount.sum()
        .over()
        .partition_by(Order::customer_id)
        .as_("customer_total")
    );

// With frame
let query = select!(Sale)
    .column(Sale::amount.avg()
        .over()
        .partition_by(Sale::product_id)
        .order_by(Sale::date.asc())
        .rows_between(-2, 0)  // ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    );
```

## Window Functions to Support

- ROW_NUMBER()
- RANK()
- DENSE_RANK()
- NTILE(n)
- LAG(expr, offset)
- LEAD(expr, offset)
- FIRST_VALUE(expr)
- LAST_VALUE(expr)
- NTH_VALUE(expr, n)

Plus aggregates with OVER:
- SUM(), AVG(), COUNT(), MIN(), MAX()

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] over() creates window function
- [ ] partition_by() works
- [ ] order_by() works
- [ ] rows_between() for frame
- [ ] range_between() for frame
- [ ] All window functions supported
- [ ] Correct SQL for each dialect

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-query/src/window.rs)
- [ ] Test ROW_NUMBER() OVER generation
- [ ] Test RANK() and DENSE_RANK()
- [ ] Test PARTITION BY clause
- [ ] Test ORDER BY within OVER
- [ ] Test frame specification (ROWS/RANGE)
- [ ] Test LAG/LEAD functions
- [ ] Test aggregate functions in OVER

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/query_window.rs)
- [ ] SELECT with ROW_NUMBER() ranks correctly
- [ ] PARTITION BY groups correctly
- [ ] Running sum with SUM() OVER
- [ ] Complex window: RANK() PARTITION BY x ORDER BY y
- [ ] Multiple window functions in same query
- [ ] Window function in subquery

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Window clause SQL generation
- [ ] TRACE: Frame specification details

 {"id":"bd-1dv","title":"Implement field alias system (alias, validation_alias, serialization_alias)","description":"## Description

Add support for field aliases matching Pydantic behavior.

## Python Behavior

```python
class User(SQLModel):
    internal_id: int = Field(alias='id')
    user_name: str = Field(
        validation_alias='username',  # Accept 'username' on input
        serialization_alias='userName'  # Output as 'userName'
    )
```

## Rust Implementation

### Macro Attributes
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct User {
    #[sqlmodel(alias = "id")]
    internal_id: i32,
    
    #[sqlmodel(
        validation_alias = "username",
        serialization_alias = "userName"
    )]
    user_name: String,
}
```

### Generated Code

For validation_alias:
- Accept the alias during deserialization
- Map to actual field name

For serialization_alias:
- Output using alias during serialization

### Serde Integration

Use #[serde(alias = "...")] for validation_alias
Use #[serde(rename = "...")] for serialization_alias

## Files to Modify

- crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/parse.rs - Parse new attributes
- crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/model.rs - Generate serde attributes
- crates/sqlmodel/tests/alias.rs - Add tests

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] alias attribute works for both input and output
- [ ] validation_alias only affects input
- [ ] serialization_alias only affects output
- [ ] Works with JSON serialization
- [ ] Works with model_validate/model_dump
- [ ] Comprehensive tests

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/field.rs)
- [ ] Test alias parsed from attribute
- [ ] Test validation_alias only used for input
- [ ] Test serialization_alias only used for output
- [ ] Test populate_by_name allows both
- [ ] Test alias conflicts detected
- [ ] Test alias with special characters

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/field_aliases.rs)
- [ ] JSON with alias name → model_validate works
- [ ] model_dump with by_alias=true uses aliases
- [ ] validation_alias + serialization_alias asymmetric
- [ ] Database column uses serialization_alias
- [ ] API round-trip with aliases

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Alias resolution during parse
- [ ] TRACE: Field name mapping decisions

 {"id":"bd-1fs","title":"Computed Fields and Hybrid Properties","description":"## Overview

Implement computed fields and hybrid properties from Pydantic/SQLAlchemy.

## Pydantic Computed Fields

```python
from pydantic import computed_field

class User(SQLModel):
    first_name: str
    last_name: str
    
    @computed_field
    @property
    def full_name(self) -> str:
        return f'{self.first_name} {self.last_name}'
```

### Behavior
- Computed at access time (not stored in DB)
- Included in model_dump() unless exclude_computed_fields=True
- Can be marked as cached
- Can have custom serialization

## SQLAlchemy Hybrid Properties

```python
from sqlalchemy.ext.hybrid import hybrid_property

class User(SQLModel):
    _password: str
    
    @hybrid_property
    def password(self):
        return self._password
    
    @password.setter
    def password(self, value):
        self._password = hash_password(value)
    
    @password.expression
    def password(cls):
        return cls._password
```

### Behavior
- Work both at Python/Rust level AND in SQL queries
- expression() method returns SQL expression for use in WHERE clauses
- Allows computed values to be used in database queries

## Rust Implementation

```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct User {
    first_name: String,
    last_name: String,
    
    #[sqlmodel(computed)]
    full_name: Computed<String>,
}

impl User {
    fn compute_full_name(&self) -> String {
        format!("{} {}", self.first_name, self.last_name)
    }
}
```

Or with hybrid properties:
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct User {
    #[sqlmodel(hybrid, sql_expr = "age * 365")]
    age_in_days: Hybrid<i32>,
}
```","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T05:00:50.645369854Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T16:58:24.853369376Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1fs","depends_on_id":"bd-162","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T16:58:24.853345732Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1fz","title":"Define RelationshipInfo struct and RelationshipKind enum","description":"# Task: Define RelationshipInfo and RelationshipKind

## Context
This is the foundational data structure for the entire relationship system. It mirrors Python's RelationshipInfo but adapted for Rust's type system.

## What to Implement

### 1. RelationshipKind Enum
```rust
// In sqlmodel-core/src/relationship.rs (new file)

/// The type of relationship between two models.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum RelationshipKind {
    /// One-to-one: Hero has one Profile
    OneToOne,
    /// Many-to-one: Many Heroes belong to one Team
    ManyToOne,
    /// One-to-many: One Team has many Heroes
    OneToMany,
    /// Many-to-many: Heroes have many Powers, Powers have many Heroes
    ManyToMany,
}
```

### 2. RelationshipInfo Struct
```rust
/// Metadata about a relationship between models.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct RelationshipInfo {
    /// Name of the relationship field
    pub name: &'static str,
    
    /// The related model's table name
    pub related_table: &'static str,
    
    /// Kind of relationship
    pub kind: RelationshipKind,
    
    /// Local foreign key column (for ManyToOne)
    /// e.g., "team_id" on Hero
    pub local_key: Option<&'static str>,
    
    /// Remote foreign key column (for OneToMany)
    /// e.g., "team_id" on Hero when accessed from Team
    pub remote_key: Option<&'static str>,
    
    /// Link table for ManyToMany relationships
    pub link_table: Option<LinkTableInfo>,
    
    /// The field on the related model that points back
    pub back_populates: Option<&'static str>,
    
    /// Whether to use lazy loading
    pub lazy: bool,
    
    /// Cascade delete behavior
    pub cascade_delete: bool,
}
```

### 3. LinkTableInfo for Many-to-Many
```rust
/// Information about a link/join table for many-to-many relationships.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct LinkTableInfo {
    /// The link table name (e.g., "hero_powers")
    pub table_name: &'static str,
    
    /// Column in link table pointing to local model
    /// e.g., "hero_id" in hero_powers
    pub local_column: &'static str,
    
    /// Column in link table pointing to remote model
    /// e.g., "power_id" in hero_powers
    pub remote_column: &'static str,
}
```

### 4. Add to Model Trait
```rust
// Extend the Model trait
pub trait Model: Sized {
    // ... existing methods ...
    
    /// Relationship metadata for this model
    const RELATIONSHIPS: &'static [RelationshipInfo] = &[];
}
```

## Files to Modify
- Create: `crates/sqlmodel-core/src/relationship.rs`
- Modify: `crates/sqlmodel-core/src/lib.rs` (export new types)
- Modify: `crates/sqlmodel-core/src/model.rs` (add RELATIONSHIPS to trait)

## Testing
- Unit tests for RelationshipKind methods
- Unit tests for RelationshipInfo builder pattern
- Ensure Default impl exists

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] RelationshipKind enum with all 4 variants
- [ ] RelationshipInfo struct with all fields
- [ ] LinkTableInfo struct
- [ ] Builder pattern for RelationshipInfo (const fn where possible)
- [ ] Model trait updated with RELATIONSHIPS constant
- [ ] All types exported from sqlmodel-core
- [ ] Unit tests pass
- [ ] Documentation with examples","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T20:14:40.670335947Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T20:39:38.877211592Z","closed_at":"2026-01-27T20:39:38.877144336Z","close_reason":"Completed: RelationshipKind/Info + Model::RELATIONSHIPS","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1fz","depends_on_id":"bd-1ak","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T20:14:40.688123478Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-3u8","title":"Implement schema extraction from Model definitions","description":"# Task: Implement Schema Extraction from Model Definitions

## Context
To compare against the database, we need to extract the "expected" schema from Rust Model definitions at compile time or runtime.

## Goal
```rust
// From these Model definitions...
#[derive(Model)]
struct Hero {
    #[sqlmodel(primary_key, auto_increment)]
    id: Option<i64>,
    
    #[sqlmodel(sql_type = "VARCHAR(100)")]
    name: String,
    
    #[sqlmodel(nullable)]
    age: Option<i32>,
    
    #[sqlmodel(foreign_key = "teams.id", on_delete = "CASCADE")]
    team_id: Option<i64>,
}

// ...extract this schema:
TableSchema {
    name: "heroes",
    columns: [
        ColumnSchema { name: "id", sql_type: "BIGINT", nullable: false, auto_increment: true },
        ColumnSchema { name: "name", sql_type: "VARCHAR(100)", nullable: false },
        ColumnSchema { name: "age", sql_type: "INTEGER", nullable: true },
        ColumnSchema { name: "team_id", sql_type: "BIGINT", nullable: true },
    ],
    primary_key: Some(PrimaryKeySchema { columns: ["id"] }),
    foreign_keys: [
        ForeignKeySchema { 
            columns: ["team_id"], 
            referenced_table: "teams",
            referenced_columns: ["id"],
            on_delete: Some("CASCADE"),
        }
    ],
    indexes: [...],
}
```

## What to Implement

### 1. Model::table_schema() Method
```rust
// Add to Model trait
pub trait Model {
    // ... existing ...
    
    /// Get the expected table schema for this model.
    fn table_schema() -> TableSchema;
}
```

### 2. Generate table_schema() in Macro
```rust
// In sqlmodel-macros/src/lib.rs

fn generate_table_schema(def: &ModelDef) -> TokenStream {
    let table_name = &def.table_name;
    
    let columns: Vec<TokenStream> = def.fields.iter().map(|f| {
        let name = &f.column_name;
        let sql_type = &f.effective_sql_type();
        let nullable = f.nullable;
        let auto_inc = f.auto_increment;
        let default = f.default.as_ref().map(|d| quote!(Some(#d.to_string())))
            .unwrap_or(quote!(None));
        
        quote! {
            sqlmodel_schema::ColumnSchema {
                name: #name.to_string(),
                sql_type: #sql_type.to_string(),
                nullable: #nullable,
                default: #default,
                auto_increment: #auto_inc,
                comment: None,
                parsed_type: sqlmodel_schema::parse_sql_type(#sql_type),
            }
        }
    }).collect();
    
    let pk_columns: Vec<String> = def.fields.iter()
        .filter(|f| f.primary_key)
        .map(|f| f.column_name.clone())
        .collect();
    
    let pk = if pk_columns.is_empty() {
        quote!(None)
    } else {
        quote! {
            Some(sqlmodel_schema::PrimaryKeySchema {
                name: None,
                columns: vec![#(#pk_columns.to_string()),*],
            })
        }
    };
    
    let fks: Vec<TokenStream> = def.fields.iter()
        .filter_map(|f| f.foreign_key.as_ref().map(|fk| (f, fk)))
        .map(|(f, fk)| {
            let col = &f.column_name;
            let (ref_table, ref_col) = parse_fk_reference(fk);
            let on_delete = f.on_delete.as_ref()
                .map(|d| quote!(Some(#d.to_string())))
                .unwrap_or(quote!(None));
            let on_update = f.on_update.as_ref()
                .map(|u| quote!(Some(#u.to_string())))
                .unwrap_or(quote!(None));
            
            quote! {
                sqlmodel_schema::ForeignKeySchema {
                    name: None,
                    columns: vec![#col.to_string()],
                    referenced_table: #ref_table.to_string(),
                    referenced_columns: vec![#ref_col.to_string()],
                    on_delete: #on_delete,
                    on_update: #on_update,
                }
            }
        })
        .collect();
    
    let indexes: Vec<TokenStream> = def.fields.iter()
        .filter_map(|f| f.index.as_ref().map(|idx| (f, idx)))
        .map(|(f, idx_name)| {
            let col = &f.column_name;
            quote! {
                sqlmodel_schema::IndexSchema {
                    name: #idx_name.to_string(),
                    columns: vec![#col.to_string()],
                    unique: false,
                    index_type: None,
                }
            }
        })
        .collect();
    
    // Unique constraints
    let uniques: Vec<TokenStream> = def.fields.iter()
        .filter(|f| f.unique)
        .map(|f| {
            let col = &f.column_name;
            quote! {
                sqlmodel_schema::UniqueConstraintSchema {
                    name: None,
                    columns: vec![#col.to_string()],
                }
            }
        })
        .collect();
    
    quote! {
        fn table_schema() -> sqlmodel_schema::TableSchema {
            sqlmodel_schema::TableSchema {
                name: #table_name.to_string(),
                columns: vec![#(#columns),*],
                primary_key: #pk,
                foreign_keys: vec![#(#fks),*],
                unique_constraints: vec![#(#uniques),*],
                indexes: vec![#(#indexes),*],
                check_constraints: vec![],
            }
        }
    }
}
```

### 3. Aggregate Multiple Models
```rust
/// Build a complete expected schema from multiple models.
pub fn expected_schema<M: Model>() -> DatabaseSchema {
    let mut schema = DatabaseSchema::new(Dialect::default());
    schema.tables.insert(M::table_name().to_string(), M::table_schema());
    schema
}

/// Build schema from a tuple of models.
pub fn expected_schema_all<Models: ModelTuple>() -> DatabaseSchema {
    let mut schema = DatabaseSchema::new(Dialect::default());
    for table in Models::all_table_schemas() {
        schema.tables.insert(table.name.clone(), table);
    }
    schema
}

// Trait for tuples of models
pub trait ModelTuple {
    fn all_table_schemas() -> Vec<TableSchema>;
}

// Implement for tuples
impl<A: Model, B: Model> ModelTuple for (A, B) {
    fn all_table_schemas() -> Vec<TableSchema> {
        vec![A::table_schema(), B::table_schema()]
    }
}

impl<A: Model, B: Model, C: Model> ModelTuple for (A, B, C) {
    fn all_table_schemas() -> Vec<TableSchema> {
        vec![A::table_schema(), B::table_schema(), C::table_schema()]
    }
}

// ... more tuple impls via macro
```

### 4. Handle Type Mapping Variations
SQL types vary by dialect:
```rust
impl ColumnSchema {
    /// Normalize the SQL type for comparison.
    /// e.g., "INT" and "INTEGER" are equivalent.
    pub fn normalized_type(&self, dialect: Dialect) -> String {
        let upper = self.sql_type.to_uppercase();
        
        match dialect {
            Dialect::Sqlite => {
                // SQLite type affinity
                if upper.contains("INT") { "INTEGER".to_string() }
                else if upper.contains("CHAR") || upper.contains("TEXT") { "TEXT".to_string() }
                else if upper.contains("REAL") || upper.contains("FLOAT") || upper.contains("DOUBLE") { "REAL".to_string() }
                else if upper.contains("BLOB") { "BLOB".to_string() }
                else { upper }
            }
            Dialect::Postgres => {
                // PostgreSQL normalizations
                match upper.as_str() {
                    "INT" | "INT4" => "INTEGER".to_string(),
                    "INT8" => "BIGINT".to_string(),
                    "INT2" => "SMALLINT".to_string(),
                    "FLOAT4" => "REAL".to_string(),
                    "FLOAT8" => "DOUBLE PRECISION".to_string(),
                    _ => upper,
                }
            }
            Dialect::Mysql => {
                // MySQL normalizations
                match upper.as_str() {
                    "INT" => "INT".to_string(),
                    "INTEGER" => "INT".to_string(),
                    _ => upper,
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

 {"id":"bd-3w42","title":"Implement model-level validators","description":"## Description

Validators that run on entire model, not just individual fields.

## Python Behavior

```python
from pydantic import model_validator

class User(SQLModel):
    password: str
    confirm_password: str
    
    @model_validator(mode='after')
    def passwords_match(self) -> Self:
        if self.password != self.confirm_password:
            raise ValueError('passwords do not match')
        return self
    
    @model_validator(mode='before')
    @classmethod
    def preprocess(cls, data: dict) -> dict:
        # Can modify data before field validation
        return data
```

## Rust Implementation

### Option 1: Trait method
```rust
pub trait ModelValidate {
    fn validate_model(&self) -> Result<(), ValidationError> {
        Ok(())
    }
}

impl ModelValidate for User {
    fn validate_model(&self) -> Result<(), ValidationError> {
        if self.password != self.confirm_password {
            return Err(ValidationError::new("passwords do not match"));
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

### Option 2: Attribute on impl
```rust
#[derive(Model, Validate)]
#[validate(model = "validate_passwords")]
struct User {
    password: String,
    confirm_password: String,
}

impl User {
    fn validate_passwords(&self) -> Result<(), String> {
        if self.password != self.confirm_password {
            return Err("passwords do not match".into());
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

## Modes

- **before**: Run before field validation, can modify input
- **after**: Run after field validation, validates relationships
- **wrap**: Wrap field validation

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] model_validator equivalent implemented
- [ ] Runs after field validation
- [ ] Can access all fields
- [ ] Can return error
- [ ] 'before' mode preprocesses input
- [ ] 'after' mode validates result

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/validate_derive.rs)

- [ ] Test model_validator mode="before" parsed
- [ ] Test validator receives full model
- [ ] Test validator can modify model

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/model_validators.rs)
- [ ] mode="after" runs after field validation
- [ ] mode="before" preprocesses input
- [ ] Validator can raise error
- [ ] Multiple model validators
- [ ] Cross-field validation (password confirm)

### Logging

- [ ] TRACE: Validation mode

 {"id":"bd-3xk","title":"Implement Session.expunge_all()","description":"## Description

Remove all objects from session without deleting from database.

## Python Behavior

```python
session.expunge_all()  # All objects detached
```

After expunge_all:
- Objects become detached
- Changes won't be persisted
- Objects can be merged back later

## Rust Implementation

```rust
impl Session {
    pub fn expunge_all(&mut self) {
        self.identity_map.clear();
        self.new.clear();
        self.dirty.clear();
        self.deleted.clear();
    }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All objects removed from session tracking
- [ ] Identity map cleared
- [ ] Pending changes discarded (but not reverted in objects)
- [ ] Objects can be merged back into new session

- [ ] expunge_all with relationships

Add full Decimal type support with precision and scale validation.

## Python Behavior

```python
from decimal import Decimal

class Product(SQLModel, table=True):
    price: Decimal = Field(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2)
    # SQL: DECIMAL(10, 2) or NUMERIC(10, 2)
```

## Current Status

We have a workaround using sql_type attribute:
```rust
#[sqlmodel(sql_type = "DECIMAL(10,2)")]
price: Decimal,
```

But need native support matching Python.

## Rust Implementation

### Macro Attributes
```rust
#[derive(Model, Validate)]
struct Product {
    #[sqlmodel(max_digits = 10, decimal_places = 2)]
    price: rust_decimal::Decimal,
}
```

### SQL Generation
Generate correct DDL:
- PostgreSQL: NUMERIC(10, 2)
- MySQL: DECIMAL(10, 2)
- SQLite: TEXT (with validation)

### Validation
- Check total digits <= max_digits
- Check decimal places <= decimal_places

 {"id":"bd-59d","title":"Create SchemaTree renderable for database structure visualization","description":"## Purpose
Implement a tree renderable that displays database schema structure in a hierarchical format, perfect for visualizing table relationships, columns, indexes, and constraints.

## Background
When users inspect database schemas, they need to see:
- Tables as tree nodes
- Columns with types and constraints as children
- Indexes and foreign keys as nested items
- Visual distinction between primary keys, nullable, indexed columns

This uses rich_rust's Tree component to create an intuitive visual representation.

## Implementation Details

### File Location
`crates/sqlmodel-console/src/renderables/schema_tree.rs`

### Core Struct
```rust
use rich_rust::prelude::*;
use sqlmodel_schema::{TableSchema, ColumnInfo, IndexInfo, ForeignKey};

/// Configuration for schema tree rendering
pub struct SchemaTreeConfig {
    /// Show column types inline
    pub show_types: bool,
    /// Show constraints (PK, FK, NOT NULL)
    pub show_constraints: bool,
    /// Show indexes
    pub show_indexes: bool,
    /// Color theme
    pub theme: Theme,
}

impl Default for SchemaTreeConfig {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            show_types: true,
            show_constraints: true,
            show_indexes: true,
            theme: Theme::default(),
        }
    }
}

/// Renders a database schema as a tree structure
pub struct SchemaTree<'a> {
    tables: &'a [TableSchema],
    config: SchemaTreeConfig,
    title: Option<String>,
}

impl<'a> SchemaTree<'a> {
    pub fn new(tables: &'a [TableSchema]) -> Self {
        Self {
            tables,
            config: SchemaTreeConfig::default(),
            title: None,
        }
    }

    pub fn with_config(mut self, config: SchemaTreeConfig) -> Self {
        self.config = config;
        self
    }

    pub fn title(mut self, title: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        self.title = Some(title.into());
        self
    }

    /// Build tree node for a single table
    fn build_table_node(&self, table: &TableSchema) -> TreeNode {
        let table_label = format\!(
            "[{}]{}[/]",
            self.config.theme.table_name_color(),
            table.name
        );
        let mut node = TreeNode::new(&table_label);

        // Add columns
        for col in &table.columns {
            let col_node = self.build_column_node(col, table);
            node.add_child(col_node);
        }

        // Add indexes if enabled
        if self.config.show_indexes {
            for idx in &table.indexes {
                let idx_node = self.build_index_node(idx);
                node.add_child(idx_node);
            }
        }

        node
    }

    fn build_column_node(&self, col: &ColumnInfo, table: &TableSchema) -> TreeNode {
        let mut parts = vec\![col.name.clone()];

        if self.config.show_types {
            parts.push(format\!(
                "[{}]{}[/]",
                self.config.theme.type_color(),
                col.sql_type
            ));
        }

        if self.config.show_constraints {
            let mut constraints = Vec::new();
            if col.is_primary_key {
                constraints.push(format\!(
                    "[{}]PK[/]",
                    self.config.theme.primary_key_color()
                ));
            }
            if \!col.nullable {
                constraints.push(format\!(
                    "[{}]NOT NULL[/]",
                    self.config.theme.constraint_color()
                ));
            }
            if col.has_default {
                constraints.push(format\!(
                    "[{}]DEFAULT[/]",
                    self.config.theme.default_color()
                ));
            }
            if \!constraints.is_empty() {
                parts.push(constraints.join(" "));
            }
        }

        TreeNode::new(&parts.join(" "))
    }

    fn build_index_node(&self, idx: &IndexInfo) -> TreeNode {
        let idx_type = if idx.unique { "UNIQUE" } else { "INDEX" };
        let label = format\!(
            "[{}]{}[/] {} ({})",
            self.config.theme.index_color(),
            idx_type,
            idx.name,
            idx.columns.join(", ")
        );
        TreeNode::new(&label)
    }

    /// Render to segments for rich mode
    pub fn render(&self, width: usize) -> Vec<Segment> {
        let root_label = self.title.clone()
            .unwrap_or_else(|| "Database Schema".to_string());
        let mut root = TreeNode::new(&format\!(
            "[bold {}]{}[/]",
            self.config.theme.heading_color(),
            root_label
        ));

        for table in self.tables {
            root.add_child(self.build_table_node(table));
        }

        let tree = Tree::new(root);
        tree.render(width)
    }

    /// Render to plain text for agent mode
    pub fn render_plain(&self) -> String {
        let mut output = String::new();
        let title = self.title.as_deref().unwrap_or("Database Schema");
        output.push_str(&format\!("{}\n", title));
        output.push_str(&"=".repeat(title.len()));
        output.push(n);

        for table in self.tables {
            output.push_str(&format\!("\nTable: {}\n", table.name));
            for col in &table.columns {
                let mut line = format\!("  - {}: {}", col.name, col.sql_type);
                if col.is_primary_key {
                    line.push_str(" [PK]");
                }
                if \!col.nullable {
                    line.push_str(" NOT NULL");
                }
                output.push_str(&format\!("{}\n", line));
            }
        }
        output
    }
}
```

## Usage Example
```rust
let tables = vec\![user_schema, post_schema];
let tree = SchemaTree::new(&tables)
    .title("my_database")
    .with_config(SchemaTreeConfig {
        show_indexes: true,
        ..Default::default()
    });

console.print_renderable(&tree);
```

## Visual Output (Rich Mode)
```
📊 my_database
├── users
│   ├── id INTEGER PK NOT NULL
│   ├── name TEXT NOT NULL
│   ├── email TEXT NOT NULL
│   └── UNIQUE idx_users_email (email)
└── posts
    ├── id INTEGER PK NOT NULL
    ├── user_id INTEGER NOT NULL
    ├── title TEXT NOT NULL
    └── INDEX idx_posts_user (user_id)
```

## Verification Steps
1. Create test schema with multiple tables
2. Verify tree renders correctly in rich mode
3. Verify plain text output for agent mode
4. Test with various config options
5. Ensure colors match Theme settings
6. Test with empty schema (no tables)
7. Test with table with no indexes

## Dependencies
- sqlmodel-schema for TableSchema, ColumnInfo types
- rich_rust Tree and TreeNode components

 {"id":"bd-751","title":"Implement discriminator for union types","description":"## Description

Support tagged unions with discriminator field.

## Python Behavior

```python
from typing import Union, Literal

class Cat(SQLModel):
    pet_type: Literal['cat'] = 'cat'
    meows: int

class Dog(SQLModel):
    pet_type: Literal['dog'] = 'dog'
    barks: int

class Pet(SQLModel):
    pet: Union[Cat, Dog] = Field(discriminator='pet_type')
```

## Rust Implementation

Use Rust enums with serde tag:
```rust
#[derive(Model, Serialize, Deserialize)]
#[serde(tag = "pet_type")]
enum Pet {
    #[serde(rename = "cat")]
    Cat { meows: i32 },
    #[serde(rename = "dog")]
    Dog { barks: i32 },
}
```

Or with structs:
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct Owner {
    #[sqlmodel(discriminator = "pet_type")]
    pet: PetUnion,
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] discriminator attribute parsed
- [ ] Correct serde tag generated
- [ ] Works with nested unions
- [ ] Validation of discriminator values

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/discriminator.rs)
- [ ] Test discriminator field parsing
- [ ] Test union type resolution
- [ ] Test discriminator in validation
- [ ] Test discriminator in serialization

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/discriminator.rs)
- [ ] Union type with discriminator
- [ ] model_validate uses discriminator
- [ ] model_dump includes discriminator
- [ ] Nested unions with discriminator
- [ ] Invalid discriminator value error

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Discriminator resolution
- [ ] TRACE: Union type matching
